coredata.dc:title: Monitoring the mechanism of anti-cancer agents to inhibit colorectal cancer cell proliferation: Enzymatic biosensing of glucose combined with molecular docking
coredata.dc:creator: Yazdanparast, Samira
Benvidi, Ali
Abbasi, Saleheh
Sabbagh, Seyyed Kazem
coredata.dc:description: Glucose, a major energy source in cellular metabolism, has a significant role in cell growth. The increase in glucose uptake is a distinguishing hallmark in cancer cells. A key step in glucose utilization is the transport of glucose to the cancer cells for supplying their additional energy. The glucose transporter (or GLUT) family is a membrane protein which facilitates the uptake of glucose in most cancer cell types. Given the increased glucose level in cancer cells and the regulatory role of GLUTs in glucose uptake, it is required to combine both experimental and theoretical studies to develop new methods to monitor cell proliferation. Herein, for the first time, a new strategy was proposed to evaluate the cell proliferation of HT-29 based on glucose consumption in the presence of resveratrol (RSV) as an anticancer agent. A hybrid nanocomposite of carbon nanofibers and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots was used to design an enzymatic sensor for the selective and sensitive determination of glucose in cancer cells. The results obtained from the voltammetric technique were compared with the conventional colorimetric assay. A good correlation was observed between the proliferation rate and glucose utilization by cancer cells. As it was observed, RSV induces a decrease in glucose consumption, indicating lower glucose uptake efficiency for HT-29 cells. Molecular docking studies reveal that RSV can block the interaction of glucose with the GLUT family. This is one of the possible mechanisms for the decrease of glucose level followed by the reduction of cell proliferation in the presence of RSV. Compared with traditional methods, in vitro electrochemical techniques benefit from simple, nontoxic, sensitive and low-cost detection assays and hence serve as a novel tool to pursue the growth inhibition of cancer cell in response to anti-cancer agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose transporter family
Cancer cell proliferation
Resveratrol
Electrochemical enzymatic sensor
Carbon nanofiber
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Glucose oxidase
Colon cancer cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the modified electrode by SEM, FT-IR and XRD methods
Direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase on CNFs-NGQDs nanocomposite
Optimization of the experimental parameters for glucose quantification
Electroanalytical sensing of glucose using the fabricated electrode
Investigation of the precision and stability of the biosensor
Glucose utilization by the HT-29 cancer cell line
Comparative results of monitoring cell proliferation with the proposed electrochemical sensor and the MTT assay
3.2.7. Effects of RSV on the proliferation of HT-29 colon cancer cells as monitored by the MTT assay and the glucose consumption biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scanning electron micrographs of (A) CNFs on the surface of a GC electrode, (B) CNFs –NGQDs nanocomposite on a GC electrode, and (C) the distribution of the GOx enzyme on the surface of a CNFs-NGQDs/GCE.
XRD patterns of (A) the synthesized NGQDs, (B) CNFs, and (C) the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized NGQDs.
Cyclic voltammograms recorded for (a) GOx/GCE, (b) NGQDs-GOx/GCE, (c) CNFs-GOx/GCE, and (d) CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCEs in PBS (pH 7.4) at the scan rate of 0.1 V s−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx-modified GCE electrode (under optimized conditions) in air-saturated PBS (pH 7.4) (a) in the absence of glucose, (b) in the presence of glucose with the concentration of 2 mmol L−1.

Differential pulse voltammograms of the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx-modified GCE electrode in 0.1 M of air-saturated PBS (pH 7.4) obtained as the glucose concentration increased from 0 to l5 mmol L−1 glucose injection. The inset shows the calibration plots of the glucose concentrations against Ip.

(A) Plot of glucose utilization versus cell number in the absence of RSV, (B) Comparison of the results obtained from an electrochemical assay based on glucose utilization and the MTT assay for the prediction of the number of cells within 0 to 72 h in the absence of RSV (Each experiment was repeated 4 times (n = 4)).

Effects of different concentrations of resveratrol on the proliferation of the HT-29 cell line at various incubation times obtained from (A) the electrochemical monitoring of glucose consumption and (B) the MTT assay (Each experiment was repeated 4 times (n = 4)).

(A) The 3D crystal structure of GLUT3 with glucose; (B) Molecular interactions between glucose and amino acid residues in the binding site; (C) The 3D crystal structure of GLUT3 with Resveratrol; (D) Molecular interactions between Resveratrol and amino acid residues in the binding site.

(A) Crystal structure of GLUT1 in a complex with cytochalasin B; (B) Binding interactions of cytochalasin B with amino acid residues in the binding site; (C) Crystal structure of GLUT1 in a complex with Resveratrol; (D) Binding interactions of Resveratrol with amino acid residues in the binding site.
Comparison between docking results of two ligands with GLUT3.
Comparison between docking results of two ligands with GLUT1.

head.ce:title: Monitoring the mechanism of anti-cancer agents to inhibit colorectal cancer cell proliferation: Enzymatic biosensing of glucose combined with molecular docking

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, 89195-741, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, 89195-741, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biology, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glucose, a major energy source in cellular metabolism, has a significant role in cell growth. The increase in glucose uptake is a distinguishing hallmark in cancer cells. A key step in glucose utilization is the transport of glucose to the cancer cells for supplying their additional energy. The glucose transporter (or GLUT) family is a membrane protein which facilitates the uptake of glucose in most cancer cell types. Given the increased glucose level in cancer cells and the regulatory role of GLUTs in glucose uptake, it is required to combine both experimental and theoretical studies to develop new methods to monitor cell proliferation. Herein, for the first time, a new strategy was proposed to evaluate the cell proliferation of HT-29 based on glucose consumption in the presence of resveratrol (RSV) as an anticancer agent. A hybrid nanocomposite of carbon nanofibers and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots was used to design an enzymatic sensor for the selective and sensitive determination of glucose in cancer cells. The results obtained from the voltammetric technique were compared with the conventional colorimetric assay. A good correlation was observed between the proliferation rate and glucose utilization by cancer cells. As it was observed, RSV induces a decrease in glucose consumption, indicating lower glucose uptake efficiency for HT-29 cells. Molecular docking studies reveal that RSV can block the interaction of glucose with the GLUT family. This is one of the possible mechanisms for the decrease of glucose level followed by the reduction of cell proliferation in the presence of RSV. Compared with traditional methods, in vitro electrochemical techniques benefit from simple, nontoxic, sensitive and low-cost detection assays and hence serve as a novel tool to pursue the growth inhibition of cancer cell in response to anti-cancer agents.

ce:section.ce:para: The term cancer refers to a large group of diseases characterized by abnormal cells that grow and invade healthy cells in the body. The most commonly diagnosed cancers originate in the pancreas, colon, breasts, skin and lungs. Colon cancer is the second most common cancer and the third main cause of death in the world [1]. Unlike the healthy cells, cancer cells need a large supply of energy for their rapid proliferation. ATP is the known molecule for the intracellular energy transfer. The Warburg effect suggests that cancer cells consume a large amount of glucose for their aerobic glycolysis in the cytosol. This is in contrast to the normal cells which mainly generate ATP through mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. Glucose metabolism is a vital physiological program which not only serves as a primary source of energy for cell proliferation but also affects the signaling pathways of cell death [2–4]. It is known that cancer cells, either benign or malignant tumor cells, have the up-regulation of glucose transporter (or GLUT) expression [5]. It has been discovered that several GLUTs are expressed at a very high level in most cancer cells [6]. Glucose transporter 1 (or GLUT1) and Glucose transporter 3 (or GLUT3) are overexpressed in different kinds of tumor. The overexpression of GLUTs is the main hallmark in numerous types of cancer. Hence, inhibition of GLUT1 and GLUT3 is a novel promising strategy for the development of medicines aimed at the treatment of cancer [7]. One of the hot topics in the research on cancer is the identification of natural polyphenol with multi-targeting effects used in the pharmacology industries. One such plant-derived polyphenol is resveratrol (RSV, 3,5,4′-trihydroxy-trans-stilbene). It is a potent chemo-preventive bioactive compound found largely in several nutritional sources such as red grapes, blueberries and peanuts [8,9]. Due to its pleiotropic effects through intracellular signaling processes, RSV has a major antitumor potential. Recently, there have been controversial reports on its autophagy function in cancer cells. It has been exhibited that RSV increases the release of several cytokines from immune cells, promoting cytotoxicity against cancer cells. Due to its antioxidant activity, RSV not only has an anti-inflammation effect but also serves as the suppressor of cancer cell survival [10]. Given the increased glucose level in cancer cells and the regulatory role of GLUTs in glucose uptake, it is required to combine both experimental and theoretical studies to develop new methods for monitoring the cell proliferation. Cell viability is the most important parameter investigated by conventional morphological techniques. The leading techniques to monitor the viability and proliferation of cells are 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), DNA fragmentation, and LDH (lactate dehydrogenase) release [11,12]. They have been widely used to study the interactions of cells with drugs. These assay methods have some common limitations such as expensive reagents, sensitivity to environmental conditions and the use of toxic or even carcinogenic dyes [13,14].

Electrochemical techniques have been used in the assessment of cell viability and the screening of anticancer drugs. In vitro electrochemical methods make simple, nontoxic, sensitive and low-cost detection assays, as compared to traditional methods [15]. On the side of the methods used to investigate the inhibitory impacts of natural compounds on cancer cells, electrochemical methods combined with molecular docking studies have little been applied to monitor the glucose level in cells. Hence, this study seeks to explore the proliferation rate of colon cancer cells in the presence of anti-cancer agents through glucose utilization measurements. Indeed, the anti-proliferative effect of RSV on cancer cells is evaluated by the tracking of cell proliferation using an electrochemical biosensor.

Electrochemical enzymatic biosensors are the most preferable tools for glucose determination owing to their unique features such as simplicity, high specificity, sensitivity, and quick response [16,17]. These biosensors specifically use glucose oxidase (GOx), which is an important glycoprotein composed of two identical subunits with two Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) coenzymes bound together. The redox center of GOx (or FAD) is deeply buried inside the thick protein shell, which hinders the electron transfer between the active sites of enzymes and the electrode surface [18,19]. Many efforts have been made to increase the electron transfer rate and the sensitivity of these biosensors. The introduction of a nano-dimensional interface for preparing a suitable enzyme immobilization matrix can be a good way of simplifying the direct electron transfer from an enzyme to the electrode surface [20]. In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been considered as promising materials for the immobilization of desired enzymes. This feature is owing to such superior properties as non-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, electro-conductivity, and aqueous solubility [21,22]. It has been reported that carbon nanotubes as a support for NGQDs can not only facilitate the electron transfer of NGQDs but also prevent large aggregates, thus leading to the good dispersion of quantum dots [23,24]. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the application of glucose biosensors combined with molecular docking studies to check the anticancer activity of RSV on cancer cells. The present study, therefore, focuses on the use of a carbon nanofibers-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (CNFs-NGQDs) hybrid interface coupled with GOx to construct an electrochemical glucose biosensor to monitor colon cancer cell proliferation. A lot of scientists have studied the mechanism of glucose transport in cancer cells. However, it is still unclear how inhibitors prevent this process. In this regard, novel molecular modeling techniques have a key role to solve many biological problems by identifying new small molecules with inhibitory effects on protein-protein or protein-ligand interactions [25–27]. Hence, a basic part of the current study is dedicated to molecular docking studies. The level of GLUT1 expression in cancer cells is correlated with their proliferative activity. Usually, GLUT3 is mainly expressed in the organs with great energy requirement to supplement GLUT1. The docking studies in this paper are based on the hypothesis that RSV can interact with GLUT1 and GLUT3 and interfere with glucose transport in cancer cells. Thus, exploring the interactions of RSV and GLUT1 along with GLU3 through molecular docking is an innovative mechanism that paves the way to find new anticancer drugs. Glucose utilization, as a marker of cell growth, can reveal the effect of anti-cancer agents on cancer cells. In this case, RSV can serve as an inhibitor of glucose transport to prevent glucose uptake. The agent is applicable to in vitro cancer cell evaluation, which is a universally practiced method of studying cancer activity.

The materials for the experiments in this study included d-(+)-glucose (>98 %), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), citric acid, urea, ethanol, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), resveratrol (RSV) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) purchased from Sigma Aldrich Corporation. In addition, Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM), Glucose oxidase (GOx, EC 1.1.3.4), fetal bovine serum (FBS), Penicillin/streptomycin and trypsin were provided from Sigma Aldrich (USA). The HT-29 colon cancer cell line was also acquired from the Iranian Biological Research Centre. High-purity nitrogen was used to obtain a controlled atmosphere in an electrochemical cell. All the reagents used in this study were of analytical grades and applied without further purification. All the solutions were freshly prepared with deionized water. Both the buffer solution and the deionized water were sterilized using an autoclave. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed with a TESCAN instrument (VEGA3 model). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out by a spectrometer (VERTEX 70, Bruker Optics, Ettlingen, Germany) equipped with a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Bruker D2 PHASER. An X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54184 Å) was used to determine the crystal phase of the obtained samples.

A green method was employed to synthesize water-soluble NGQDs using citric acid, which is a natural antioxidant available in fruits and vegetables. This approach is based on simple economic strategies. Briefly, in a typical process of synthesis, citric acid as a source of carbon and urea as a source of nitrogen (1:4 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water. The solution was placed in an electric oven and heated at 140 °C for three hours. The solid product was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and then it was rinsed with ethanol. Finally, a brown powder of NGQDs was obtained after it was dried up at 50 °C.

In this study, prior to the preparation of a modified working electrode, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished sequentially with alumina slurry in sizes of 1, 0.3 and 0.05 μm to obtain a mirror-like surface. After each polishing step, the mirror-like GCE was rinsed thoroughly with anhydrous alcohol and distilled water. Next, the pre-cleaned electrode was consecutively sonicated in ethanol and double distilled water for five minutes and then allowed to dry in ambient conditions. Prior to this pretreatment, CNFs, NGQDs and a 1:1 mixture of CNFs and NGQDs were dispersed in a nafion solution (0.05 %) by ultrasonic agitation in separate tubes for an hour. Then, a solution of GOx (10 mg mL−1) was added to the suspension containing CNFs and NGQDs. Five μL of the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx solution was cast on the surface of a freshly cleaned GCE. The fabricated electrode was named a CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE. In a similar procedure, 5 μL of CNFs and 5 μL of NGQDs solutions were deposited each on the surface of a freshly polished GCE. The electrodes obtained were named CNFs/GCE and NGQDs/GCE respectively.

A human colon carcinoma cell line (HT-29) was grown in a Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM). All the mediums were supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin. The cells were incubated at 37 °C with 95 % of air and 5 % of CO2. The treated and the control groups of HT-29 cells were cultured in the DMEM containing a certain concentration of resveratrol and DMSO respectively. Twenty four hours before the RSV treatment, the cells were trypsinized and plated at the confluency of 70–80 %. At each point of the experiments, the spent medium was used for electrochemical studies. The MTT assay is used to determine the cellular viability, and the cell density was estimated with a hemocytometer.

An MTT assay is a valuable tool for the quantitative assessment of cell proliferation and the growth rate of cancer cells. The inhibitory effect of RSV on cell growth (or the viability of the HT-29 cell lines) was measured using this assay, which actually evaluated the viable cells. The HT-29 cancer cells were seeded in 96-well plates. Then, the plates were incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Different concentrations of RSV (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 ppm) were added to the wells incubated over the time points of 0–72 h. Four wells were left untreated as the control group for the MTT assay. The cells were then counted using a standard technique, and the cell proliferation was assessed by the control counts.

The PGSTAT-302 N model of an autolab potentiostat/galvanostat instrument with PGSTAT-302 N model (Eco Chemie, Netherlands) was applied in all the electrochemical measurements. The instruments were supported with the NOVA 1.7 software. To conduct the electrochemical experiments, at least three electrodes were needed. An Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0 M) (0.5 mm in diameter) was used as a reference electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode (CE, 0.5 mm in diameter) and a modified glassy carbon electrode (modified GCE, 2 mm in diameter) as a working electrode. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were carried out for the objective evaluation of glucose utilization by cancer cells, which is a marker of cell viability.

Molecular docking is performed in mechanistic studies by setting up a small molecule in the binding site of a target protein (receptor) to know how ligands inhibit the target [28]. The procedures of molecular docking in this study are explained in the following subsections.

The three-dimensional crystal structure of GLUT3 (PDB-ID: 4ZW9) and GLUT1 (PDB-ID: 5EQG), as the target proteins for docking, were retrieved from the Protein Data Bank (http://www.rcsb.org/) [29,30]. Before docking, the target proteins were prepared in such a way that the crystallographic water molecules were removed from the macromolecule files. Also, Gasteiger charges and polar hydrogen atoms were added to the final macromolecule structures.

HyperChem, a molecular modeling environment, was employed to generate a 3D structure of RSV. The geometry optimization was done by the PM3 semi-empirical method. All the calculations were performed using the Polak-Ribiere algorithm until the energy of the molecules converged to the root mean square gradient of 0.01 kcal/(Å mole).

Docking studies were conducted via the AutoDock 4.0 software, the Lamarckian genetic algorithm (LGA) and GA search. The center of a grid box was placed in the target proteins so as to build energy grids within a cubic box around the centroid of the co-crystallized ligand. The boundary box dimensions were 87 × 54 × 102 Å for GLU1 and 77 × 79 × 95 Å for GLUT3 with the grid-point spacing of 0.375 Å. Default parameters were set for the docking procedure, except 100 pose of ligands were generated to make the docking of ligand to both of proteins. The best conformation of RSV was selected as a structure with the lowest energy and a ligand with high affinity to the target macromolecules.

To confirm the docking protocol and the obtained binding modes of ligand, the docking methods were validated by re-docking experiments. A co-crystallized ligand was extracted from a protein structure available in the protein data bank, and a redock experiment was performed. Then, the ligand was docked to the native protein. The docking procedure was evaluated by the calculation of RMSD for the experimentally determined position and the position obtained from the docking of the ligand. An RMS value lower than 2 Å for the experimental and the docked co-crystallized ligand confirmed the validation of the docking method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the modified electrode by SEM, FT-IR and XRD methods
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the modified electrodes was investigated using SEM, FT-IR and XRD techniques. Fig. 1 shows the SEM images obtained for the (A) CNFs/GCE, (B) CNFs–NGQDs/GCE and (C) CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE electrodes. As it can be seen, carbon nanofiber is in the form of a helicoidal structure with an average diameter of 120−200 nm and an average length on the scale of tenths of nanometers (Fig. 1A). It provides a large surface area for the immobilization of GOx at the surface of GCE. The SEM image of CNFs-NGQDs/GCE in Fig. 1B, compared to that of CNFs/GCE, shows the synergistic impacts of CNFs and NGQDs to enhance the surface area of the modified electrode. Also, according to the SEM images, the modification of GCE with CNFs –NGQDs can be carried out well, leading to the immobilization of the enzyme on the electrode surface (Fig. 1C).

The crystalline structure and the interlayer distances of the synthesized NGQDs and CNFs were characterized by the XRD technique. Fig. 2 A shows a specific diffraction peak at 2θ = 27° which corresponds to the graphene (002) plane with a typical graphitic interlayer structure. It is in good agreement with previously reported results [31], confirms the disordered stacking of N-GQDs. Also, it introduces additional defects and more active sites along the edges of GQDs in the hydrothermal process. As shown in Fig. 2B, the XRD pattern of CNFs has two specific peaks at 2θ = 26° and 2θ = 44° belonging to the (002) and (100) planes of the carbonic structures respectively. The sharper peak shows a higher order in structure, and the wide one suggests a smaller structure. A comparison of the XRD patterns of CNFs and N-GQDs reveals the broadening of the (002) signal at 2θ = 26° of NGQDs due to its smaller size. An increase in the interlayer spacing of NGQDs strongly depends on their oxidation degree, i.e., the attached functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxylic acid) distributed on the basal plane.

Moreover, the FT-IR technique was applied to check the synthesis process of NGQDs. The FT-IR spectra of NGQDs are presented in Fig. 2C. A broad peak at ∼3446 cm−1 is ascribed to the OH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1714 cm−1 and 1187 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibrations of CO and CO. The NH in-plane stretching of nitrogen-containing groups appeared at 3215 cm−1. The peak centered at 1618 cm−1 is attributed to the CONH bonds. Also, the peaks at 1340 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 are attributed to CN stretching vibrations and the in-plane stretching of amide-carbonyl. The obtained results are indicated the amide functional groups grafted onto the graphitic sheet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase on CNFs-NGQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: It is very important to investigate the direct electron transfer mechanism in redox enzymes and the biorecognition of enzyme substrates. Thus, the electrochemical behavior of several designed electrodes immobilized with GOx was studied using cyclic voltammetry in deoxygenated PBS (0.1 M with of pH 7.4) at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Fig. 3 shows the CV responses of the GOx immobilized on the (a) GOx/GCE, (b) NGQDs-GOx/GCE, (c) CNFs-GOx/GCE, and (d) CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE. The cyclic voltammogram of GOx recorded at GCE does not show any obvious peak in the applied range of potentials. It only shows a typical capacitive CV response caused by their double layer charging (Fig. 3, curve a) [14]. To promote enzyme biosensors, there are some issues about their designing to resolve. Among them, the increase of efficiency, stability and reliability is very important [32]. The redox center of GOx (or FAD) is deeply buried inside the thick shell of the protein, which hinders the electron transfer between the active site of the enzyme and the electrode surface [22,33]. The direct electron transfer (DET) of GOx did not occur on the enzyme-modified GCE, resulting in no obvious electrochemical response of the fabricated electrode. After the surface of GCE was modified with NGQDs, there were a pair of weak redox peaks to observe for the NGQDs-GOx/GCE electrode with the anodic and cathodic peak potentials at 0.375 and 0.461 V respectively. (Fig. 3, curve b). The peak-to-peak separation and the formal potential were found to be 86 mV and 0.418 V. Due to their conducting network, NGQDs exhibited better electrical conductivity, leading to the facilitation of the DET of the enzyme. When the GCE electrode was coated with CNFs, the anodic and cathodic peak currents notably increased but ΔEp decreased, compared to the NGQDs-GOx/GCE electrode (Fig. 3, curve c). The decrease in ΔEp and the increase in the peak current suggested that the electron transfer to the active center of the enzyme was improved in the case of CNFs-GOx/GCE. CNFs possess an effective conducting network. Hence, the electrical conductivity of the modified electrode could be improved significantly, leading to the acceleration of DET.

Furthermore, the cyclic voltammograms of the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE electrode reveals a pair of well-defined redox peaks with Epa and Epc at 0.409 V and 0.447 V respectively, corresponding to 38 mV for ΔEp and 0.428 V for the formal potential. Compared to the other modified electrodes, the highest value of the anodic and cathodic currents and the lowest value of ΔEp accounted for the improvement of the electrochemical performances and the signal amplification of the fabricated electrode (Fig. 3, curve d). The formal potential value of GOx on CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE was in accordance with the typical characteristics of GOx electrochemistry in a neutral-pH solution [34], suggesting the reduction and oxidation of the FAD/FADH2 electroactive center present in the GOx enzyme. The preferential DET ability of the GOx enzyme at the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE interface is attributed to the large specific surface area, high electrical conductivity and good biocompatibility of CNFs-NGQDs nanocomposite. Since CNFs have a much larger surface area, their active surface-to-volume ratio is much higher than that of other materials. Also, N-GQDs have several types of functional groups such as carboxylic acid. These two properties contribute to the selective immobilization and stabilization of GOx on the surface of CNFs-NGQDs, benefiting the fast electron transfer to the enzyme. The electroactive protein density (Γ) of GOx on the surface of a CNFs-NGQDs-modified GC electrode can be estimated based on the formula Γ= Q/nFA, where Q is the charge involved in the reaction, n is the number of the transferred electrons, F is Faraday constant, and A is the geometric area of the electrode. Assuming 2 for n, the value of Γ was found to be 2.2 × 10−9 mol cm−2, which was higher than that of CNFs-GOx/GCE [35]. These results demonstrate the better DET capability of GOx on the CNFs-NGQDs hybrid material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental parameters for glucose quantification
ce:section.ce:para: The redox reaction of the FAD center in the active site of the GOx enzyme is a proton-coupled electron transfer reaction [36–38]. To achieve the appropriate electrochemical performance of the designed sensor, different interrelated parameters were optimized. Thus, the experimental conditions which influenced the analytical sensing of glucose were optimized. They included the concentration of the CNFs-NGQDs nanocomposite, concentration of GOx, temperature, and pH value. More information about the optimization of the experimental parameters is provided in the electronic supporting information section (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalytical sensing of glucose using the fabricated electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical performance of the fabricated biosensor was studied using cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 4 presents the CVs obtained at the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE in the air-saturated PBS (pH 7.4) in the absence (curve a) and presence of glucose (curve b). The CV responses proved that the dissolved oxygen was reduced at the designed sensor. This process was catalyzed using the GOx immobilized at the electrode surface according to the following equations [39]:
(1) GOx(FAD)+2e− + 2H ↔ GOx(FADH2)
(2) GOx(FADH2) + O2 ↔ GOx(FAD)+H2O2

The GOx immobilized on the surface of the fabricated electrode took part in an exchange of two electrons and two protons, which is a rapid electron transfer (see Eq. 1). GOx(FADH2) catalyzed the dissolved oxygen to yield the oxidized form of the enzyme, namely GOx(FAD), which underwent a reduction process (see Eq. 2). As it was seen, in the presence of oxygen, the oxidized form of flavin (FAD) was regenerated at the surface of the modified electrode. This dramatically enhanced the reduction peak current of FAD.

As a specified concentration of glucose was added to the air-saturated solution, the current of the reduction peak decreased obviously (curve b in comparison to curve a). It implies that the electrocatalytic reaction at the electrode surface was limited because of the following enzyme catalysis [40,41]:
(3) Glucose + GOx(FAD)→gluconolactone + GOx (FADH2)

Upon the addition of glucose to the air-saturated buffer solution, the cathodic peak current decreased, while the anodic peak current increased (curve b). As it is displayed in reaction 3, the biocatalytic reaction of glucose and GOx involves the oxidation of the glucose substrate. During this enzyme-catalyzed reaction, the redox center of GOx (FAD) is reduced to GOx (FADH2), and the concentration of GOx (FAD) is decreased. Hence, an increase in the glucose concentration leads to a decrease in the cathodic current of GOx (FAD) in a certain concentration range.

Under the optimal conditions obtained for the experiments, the designed biosensor was applied to quantify glucose in cancer cells. To indicate the relationship between the reduction current obtained from the electrocatalytic reaction and glucose concentration, differential pulse voltammograms were recorded at CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE. Fig. 5 , shows the curves of the differential pulse voltammogram (DPVs) obtained at the fabricated electrode upon consecutive addition of the glucose solution to the air saturated PBS (pH 7.4). There was a gradual decrease in the reduction current when the glucose concentration increased from 0 mmol L−1 to 15 mmol L−1 (Fig. 5). The corresponding calibration plot of the DPV peak currents versus the glucose concentrations is shown in the inset of Fig. 5; one can see proper linearity in the calibration range from 0 mmol L−1 to 15 mmol L−1. The linear dependency of the calibration plot is represented in the equation of Ip (μA) = (0.604) ([colucose] (mM))- 15.72 with a correlation coefficient of 0.996.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the designed biosensor was 0.15 mmol L−1. The blood glucose concentration in non-diabetic conditions had normal levels ranging from 3.9 to 7.1 mmol L−1. Under hyperglycemic conditions, in which a large quantity of glucose circulates in the blood plasma, the blood glucose level rises to higher values such as 15–20 mmol L−1. Hence, the proposed strategy has a wide detection range for the physiological sensing of glucose [14,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the precision and stability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, inter-assay and intra-assay were carried out to test the reproducibility and repeatability of the CNFs-NGQDs-GOx/GCE biosensor in the presence of glucose. The inter-assay was performed for ten measurements with the same enzymatic electrode. The reproducibility of the biosensor was studied using five parallel electrodes made for target detection. The results were analyzed based on the relative standard deviation (RSD) values. For single-electrode repeatability and electrode-to electrode reproducibility, the RSD values were found to be 2.5 % and 4.2 %. This proved that the enzymatic electrode was reproducible and the immobilization of the enzyme on the electrode was repeatable. To investigate the electrode stability, the modified electrode was stored at room temperature. Then, after three days, one week and two weeks, the current of DPVs recorded for glucose oxidation was found to be 97 %, 94 % and 92 % of the initial response respectively. It reveals the high stability of the designed electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose utilization by the HT-29 cancer cell line
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer cells require more energy to accelerate its out-of-control growth. Actually, they prefer to get their energy through glycolysis and glucose uptake. Cancer Cells reduce the ability of normal cells to consume glucose, hence taking a better share of the glucose supplement for own growth. These cells are dependent on increased glycolytic flux, which raises glucose uptake as a major index for monitoring the number of cells [43]. The designed biosensor was successfully applied to monitor the consumption of glucose by cancer cells in the absence and the presence of RSV at different time points up to 72 h. For this purpose, the HT-29 colorectal cancer cell line was used. A cell culture medium also used to determine the glucose level in the cancer cells, and electrochemical experiments were performed at specified times. The currents obtained through DPV were applied to determine the glucose concentration on the basis of glucose utilization by different numbers of cancer cells in the absence and the presence of RSV. Through electrochemical measurements, the glucose level was directly monitored in different numbers of cancer cells. The corresponding graphs are presented in Fig. 6 A. A standard curve was also drawn to evaluate the number of cancer cells on the basis of glucose utilization.

The time-dependent curve of the cell number was plotted to evaluate the effect of incubation time on cell proliferation. Also, more detailed information was provided about the number of cells in the absence of RSV. Furthermore, there was a gradual increase of cancer cells in the control group in a time-dependent manner. In other words, as the time went on, the cell proliferation increased (Fig. 6B, red color). Obviously, for the control solution, the increase in the number of cancer cells was directly related to their uptake of glucose. Thus, those cells consume excessive glucose to provide energy for their rapid growth and proliferation. Because of the fast growth of cancer cells, the time-based increase occurred in the number of those cells that were not treated with RSV (Fig. 6B, red color).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative results of monitoring cell proliferation with the proposed electrochemical sensor and the MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: Time-dependent cell proliferation was monitored by a conventional MTT assay and the novel electrochemical method developed in this study. The curve obtained from the MTT assay shows that the number of cells would increase as the time went on in the absence of RSV (Fig. 6B, blue color). The trend of quantification was pursued from 12 to maximally 72 h (Fig. 6B, blue color). The same trend was observed in the analysis of cell proliferation and cell number based on the consumption of glucose (Fig. 6B, red color). The analyses were conducted with the fabricated electrode. As it can be seen in Fig. 6B, an increase was recorded by both the glucose biosensor and the MTT assay for the cells untreated with RSV. The number of cells estimated through the electrochemical monitoring of glucose consumption was consistent with the results of the MTT assay. In addition, the curve of glucose utilization versus the number of cells (Fig. 6B, red color) turned out to be similar to the growth plot by the MTT assay (Fig. 6B, blue color). In both cases, it is understood that, when the number of cancer cells increases, a large portion of glucose is consumed for cell proliferation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.2.7. Effects of RSV on the proliferation of HT-29 colon cancer cells as monitored by the MTT assay and the glucose consumption biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the impact of RSV on the proliferation of cancer cells is examined using the proposed strategy. As observed, RSV induces a decrease in the glucose uptake and its consumption using cancer cells. Fig. 7 A plots glucose consumption versus time. Tumor cells need more glucose than normal cells to meet their energy demand. In the presence of RSV, less glucose is available for growth the cancer cells compared to their required energy. Indeed, the result has proved that RSV possesses an anticancer property by decreasing glucose consumption with an increase of incubation time (Fig. 7A,). The time-dependent monitoring of cell proliferation using the MTT assay in the presence of RSV also showed a decrease in the cell number (Fig. 7B). Thus, as colorectal cancer cells take up less glucose, they fail to grow and die. The apoptosis takes place when glucose pathways are blocked. In time-based treatment with RSV (0−72 h), most of the tested concentrations were effective in the inhibition of cell proliferation, especially after 24 h.

As it can be seen in Fig. 7A, when the RSV concentration rose from 25 to 200 ppm, a significant decrease occurred in the glucose consumption. As a result, the tumor growth had a significant decline. Also, according to Fig. 7B, incubation with RSV in the concentration of 200 ppm led to a more considerable decrease of cancer cells than in the other tested concentrations. In the concentration of 25 ppm, RSV slightly inhibited the glucose utilization by colon cancer cells (Fig. 7A). Indeed, the inhibitory effect of 25 ppm RSV is the lowest within 72 h of incubation (Fig. 7B). This implies the greatest cancer cell proliferation in this case. It is also found that the inhibition effect of RSV against cancer cell proliferation is stronger in 150 ppm than in the other tested concentrations but still lower than 250 ppm RSV. As shown in Fig. 7A, after the HT-29 cell line was treated with RSV, the glucose consumption decreased due to a rise in the concentration of the drug from 25 ppm to 200 ppm and the incubation time from 0–72 h. Thus, the number of cancer cells decreased when the incubation time and the concentration of RSV increased. Generally, the results point to the dose-dependency and time-dependency of the inhibition of colon cancer cells with RSV. The monitoring of glucose consumption using electrochemical assays reveals that RSV inhibits cancer cell proliferation in a concentration and time-dependent manner. The use of the MTT assay in the concentration range of 25−200 ppm over 0−72 h of incubation with RSV yielded results comparable to those obtained from the sensor designed for glucose consumption. As it was observed, the glucose consumption decreased along with a reduction in the cell number in the presence of RSV. The fall shown in the plot of glucose consumption versus time was similar to the typical descending curve of the cell number obtained from the MTT assay. In the presence of RSV, the number of cells decreased because of the reduction in the glucose utilization (Fig. 7A and B).

The results obtained from the electrochemical method devised in this study were completely consistent with the findings through the MTT assay. Although the MTT method is widely used, it has some disadvantages. For example, it sometimes comes up with a great variation in a linear calibration range, which may be because different experimental parameters affect the results [14]. It is also known that several chemical compounds can interfere with the MTT assay [43]. They are the redox substances which reduce MTT to formazan through a non-enzymatic reaction. Thus, they can alter the assay results [44]. In addition, the MTT reagents used to estimate cell viability have cytotoxic effects which can damage or even kill the cells during an experiment [45]. By and large, in contrast to the MTT method, the electrochemical strategy proposed in this paper is capable of evaluating the cell proliferation easily, economically, reliably and reproducibly.

Nowadays, two main approaches are applied in cancer therapy. The first is a traditional approach based on the conventional chemotherapy. The second is a novel approach based on the design of drugs to block the specific protein involved in the signaling pathways of cancer [46]. In this regard, multi-target drugs such as tyrosine kinase inhibitors and the inhibitors of mTORC1 (mammalian target of rapamycin complex 1) have significantly contributed to the improvement of conditions for the patients suffering from different tumors [47]. As described above, tumors need an increased glucose uptake and expression of GLUTs. The considerable dependence of cancer cells on glucose uptake and the high expression of GLUTs have formed a basis to design drugs which can kill tumor cells by inhibiting GLUs. The results obtained from the electrochemical experiments in this study indicate that glucose consumption decreases in cancer cells in the presence of RSV. Since the overexpression of GLUT1 and GLU3 in colon cancer cells leads to increased glucose uptake, the aim of this study has partly been to investigate the inhibitory effect of RSV on these targets using molecular docking. The following subsections are dedicated to the experiments in this regard.

To verify the results obtained from molecular docking studies, the docking protocol was validated through redocking experiments. In this way, glucose (co-crystallized ligand) was docked to the structure of GLUT3 to investigate the capability of the AutoDock program to reproduce the orientation and location of the ligand viewed in the crystal structure. The low RMSE (0.71 Å) between the experimental and docked ligand suggested that glucose in the docked model had the same binding orientation as the crystal structure. This result shows that the docking model is valid. According to this model, glucose binds to amino acids within the central cavity (Fig. 8 A and B).

A different type of residue contributes to the interaction of glucose and GLUT3 protein. Certain polar residues including Gln280, Gln281, Asn286, Asn315, Glu378, Trp386 and Gln159 interact with the ring oxygen and the OH group of glucose through hydrogen bonds. Moreover, the carbon backbone of the glucose ring is surrounded by such hydrophobic residues as Ile 162, Phe 24, Ile 285, Phe 377, Ile 166 and Phe 289 (Fig. 8B). According to the results gained from the molecular docking studies, glucose can bind with the GLUT3 structure through strong hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. It is also understood that RSV occupies the binding site of glucose on GLUT3, and the specific binding region overlaps the binding pocket of the ligand (Fig. 8A and B). The interaction between RSV and GLUT3 was explored in detail as shown in Fig. 8C and D. The docking of RSV in the binding site of GLUT3 involved the same position and interaction as that of glucose in a hydrogen bond to Gln 159, Trp 386 and Gln 280. RSV was also predicted to form hydrophobic interactions with the hydrophobic residues of GLUT3 such as Ile 162, Phe 24, Ile 285 and Phe 377 (Fig. 8D). They overlapped the reported residues in the binding site of glucose in the structure of GLUT3 (Fig. 8B). In addition, the potential binding affinity of RSV was evaluated using the Auto Dock program. RSV had a ΔGBinding value of −7.56 kcal mol−1, representing a potentially higher binding affinity than glucose with the ΔGBinding of −6.01 kcal mol−1 (Table 1 ). As a matter of fact, RSV competes with glucose at the central binding site and prevents it from entering this place. Therefore, the access of cancer cells to glucose is reduced, and glucose consumption is discouraged (Fig. 7A).
It can be concluded that RSV enters the same binding sites of GLUT3 and GLUT1 as glucose does in order to compete with it. It even prevents the entry of ligand in these parts of the proteins.

Because of the competitive interaction of RSV and glucose, the ligand RSV is inserted into the binding site on GLUT3, which is the only channel for glucose to enter certain binding areas (Fig. 8A–D). Also, RSV can block the transport cavity of GLUT1 (Fig. 9A–D). It can block the interaction of glucose with GLUT3 and GLUT1. Therefore, less amount of glucose is provided for cancer cells. Thus, glucose consumption and cell proliferation process slow down with decreasing of the most important source of energy (Fig. 7A and B). The electrochemical sensing response of GOx is also decreased in the presence of RSV, which is due to a decrease in the glucose transfer and its consumption by cancer cells. As it can be seen in Fig. 7A, with an increase in the concentration of the inhibitor, the glucose utilization gradually decreases. When the concentration of RSV is increased, more inhibitors enter the glucose binding site on GLUT3 and GLUT1, and less amount of glucose comes into this site. Therefore, the inhibition of glucose transport by GLUT1 and GLUT3 causes a reduction in glucose consumption and cell proliferation. According to the experimental results, RSV does the task of inhibition in a concentration-dependent manner, which is proved by the theoretical results. Glucose transport and its consumption would be blocked by the inhibition of such enzymes as GLUT1 and GLUT3 in the presence of RSV. They all regulate the uptake of glucose by cancer cells. Briefly, as in Fig. 7A, the strategy introduced in this study can successfully reduce the glucose level in cancer cells. The inhibition of glucose consumption pathways through this strategy results in decreased cell proliferation and tumor growth (Fig. 7B). In this regard, blocking GLUT3, GLUT1 and other GLUTs by means of RSV is one of the possible mechanisms to decrease cell proliferation. Based on the rank scores obtained from the docking studies, RSV has approximately 1.20 times more affinity than glucose for GLUT3.

It is concluded that the hydrogen bond and the hydrophobic interaction between RSV and GLUT3 (GLUT1) residues in binding sites account for the inhibitory function of RSV. Therefore, this ligand can be considered as a proper inhibitor of GLUT3, GLUT1 and other GLUTs in the treatment of cancer and cell proliferation based on glucose consumption.

In this study, a novel glucose biosensor was developed based on the nanocomposite films of CNFs-NGQDs and GOx for the sensitive detection of glucose. For the determination of glucose, the designed electrode was found to have a proper range of detection up to 15 mmol L−1 and high sensitivity. This sensing strategy was successfully used to quantify the glucose consumption in the HT-29 cell line as a new method to monitor the proliferation of colon cancer cells in the presence of resveratrol. An electrochemical assay for glucose detection can overcome some limitations of the conventional dye-based assays used to measure cancer cell proliferation. The glucose level in cancer cells is reduced in the presence of RSV. Moreover, the blockage of glucose consumption pathways accounts for a decrease in cell proliferation and tumor growth. The monitoring of glucose consumption with the fabricated electrochemical assay revealed that RSV inhibits cancer cell proliferation in concentration- and time-dependent manners. The results obtained from the MTT assay in various concentrations over different periods of incubation with RSV were comparable to the results of glucose consumption obtained from the proposed sensor. Also, the docking results indicated that the inhibition of GLUT1 and GLUT3 for the transport of glucose would result in the reduction of glucose consumption and cell proliferation. These findings suggest that the dependence of cancer cell proliferation on glucose utilization can be used as a diagnostic assay to evaluate the proliferation rate and decide on therapeutic interventions.
Samira Yazdanparast: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing. Ali Benvidi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Editing. Saleheh Abbasi: Software. Seyyed Kazem Sabbagh: Editing, Performing experiments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Yazd University Research Council (Yazd, Iran) for the financial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enzmictec.2021.109804.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
